text.title: HATEOAS, Pagination, Filtering, Idempotency
text.summary: HATEOAS links, cursor vs offset pagination, consistent filter parameters, bulk operation patterns, and idempotency keys for POST.

rule.statement: Include _links only when clients actually follow them; don't invest in full hypermedia if clients ignore links and hardcode paths anyway.
rule.rationale: HATEOAS is the ideal but rarely justified in practice — most client teams hardcode paths regardless. YAGNI applies; add links only when a client will use them.

rule.statement: Pick one pagination strategy globally — cursor for streams, offset for finite listings — and use consistent parameter names (?limit, ?cursor or ?offset).
rule.rationale: Per-endpoint pagination parameters make API clients maintain per-endpoint pagination logic. One strategy, one parameter set, applied everywhere.

example.code:
# Offset (finite, sortable): GET /users?limit=20&offset=0
# Cursor (stream, stable): GET /events?limit=20&cursor=eyJpZCI6MTIzfQ

rule.statement: Use query params for filtering (?status=active) and sorting (?sort=-created_at for descending). Use ?fields=a,b,c for sparse fieldsets.
rule.rationale: Consistent filter and sort conventions mean SDK generators and LLM agents can infer the pattern from one example and apply it to all endpoints.

rule.statement: Document and enforce Idempotency-Key headers on POST endpoints that charge, create orders, or send messages; without them retries cause double-creates.
rule.rationale: Network failures make retries necessary. Without idempotency keys, a client that retried a timed-out order creation will create two identical orders.

rule.statement: Limit sub-resource nesting to two levels (/a/{id}/b/{id}); anything deeper is a code smell — flatten with query params.
rule.rationale: Deep nesting (/a/{id}/b/{id}/c/{id}) creates excessively long, hard-to-maintain URLs. LLM agents readily produce three+ levels of nesting.
